summary:
The Spanish Conquest of the Inca Empire (1532–1572 AD) was a stunning demonstration of how a handful of European soldiers, armed with superior weapons, epidemic disease, and extraordinary audacity, could topple the largest indigenous empire in the Americas. Francisco Pizarro arrived on the Peruvian coast in 1532 with only 168 men — but the [[event:99|Inca Empire]] was already weakened by a catastrophic smallpox epidemic (which preceded the Spanish by years, killing Emperor Huayna Capac around 1527) and a bitter civil war between his sons Huascar and Atahualpa. Pizarro moved inland and at the city of Cajamarca engineered one of history's most audacious gambles: ambushing Atahualpa and his thousands of attendants in a confined plaza, killing some 2,000 Inca warriors with cavalry and firearms, and [[event:3627|capturing the emperor himself]] — a deed that paralyzed the leaderless Inca state.

Atahualpa, negotiating for his freedom, offered to fill his prison room with gold and twice over with silver — the famous Ransom of Cajamarca, the largest ransom in history. Pizarro accepted the gold (an estimated 13,000 pounds of gold objects, melted down) and then had Atahualpa executed in 1533, installing a puppet Inca. Spanish forces seized Cusco the same year. Resistance continued for decades — most dramatically through the Neo-Inca state at Vilcabamba under Manco Inca and his successors — until the final Inca ruler, Tupac Amaru I, was captured and executed in 1572. The conquest's consequences mirrored the Mexican case: demographic catastrophe (the Andean population fell from perhaps 9 million to under 600,000 by 1620), the destruction of the Inca state's redistributive economy and infrastructure, and the imposition of the mita forced labor system in the silver mines of [[event:2425|Potosí]].

read_more_links.title: Francisco Pizarro conquest Peru Inca Empire
